title: Cat Growling When Touched Near Hips: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat growling when touched near the hips? Learn about potential causes like arthritis or injury and when you should contact a veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Growling when touched near the hips is a sign of localized pain or discomfort and typically requires a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours. Seek emergency care immediately if your cat is unable to walk or has completely lost their appetite.

What does it mean if my cat growls when I touch their hips?

When a cat growls specifically when touched near the hips, it is a primary indicator of localized pain or physical discomfort. Potential causes include:

  • Degenerative joint disease (arthritis)
  • Soft tissue injuries
  • Hidden abscesses from a bite or scratch

Is my cat growling near their hips a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for this symptom is generally Medium, though certain signs require immediate intervention:

  • Within 24 to 48 hours: Standard evaluation for cats that are still mobile and eating.
  • Immediate Emergency: If the growling is accompanied by an inability to walk, dragging of the hind limbs, or a total loss of appetite.

How can photos and videos of my cat help the veterinarian with triage?

Because cats often hide pain at the clinic due to adrenaline, home documentation provides an invaluable diagnostic look at their natural behavior. Useful media includes:

  • A short clip of your cat walking or jumping in a relaxed environment.
  • A recording of their specific reaction when the hip area is approached.
  • A photo of the area to help identify subtle swelling or skin abnormalities hidden by fur.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Cats growling when touched near the hips may be exhibiting pain due to underlying conditions such as neuropathic pain, dermatologic conditions, or myopathies. Focal seizures can also present with similar signs. Alternatively, the behavior could stem from fear, potentially rooted in genetic factors, insufficient early socialization, fear-evoking experiences (e.g., unfamiliar people, cats, dogs, noises, car rides, veterinary visits), or even traumatic early experiences. Diagnostic efforts should include excluding other causes of lower urinary tract disease, such as obstruction, infection, neoplasia, or urolithiasis, potentially utilizing radiographs, ultrasonography, urinalysis, urine culture, and cystoscopy. A therapeutic trial for neuropathic pain, seizures, pruritus, or compulsive disorders might also be considered. Management should focus on identifying and avoiding stimuli that trigger the behavior and addressing any underlying medical conditions.

Chapter: Neurology, Dermatology, Psychiatry

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1574)

faqs.question: Could my cat have arthritis?
faqs.answer: Yes, osteoarthritis is extremely common in older cats and frequently affects the hip joints, causing significant pain during physical contact.

faqs.question: What should I do if my cat tries to bite when I touch their hips?
faqs.answer: Stop touching the sensitive area immediately. This is a defensive reaction to pain. Keep your cat in a quiet, low-stress environment until your vet appointment.

faqs.question: Are there other signs of hip pain I should look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for a 'bunny-hopping' gait when running, a reluctance to jump onto high surfaces, or a decrease in grooming habits around the hindquarters.
